story:
Stanislaw and his son Daniel meet in the blue gloom of an abandoned cinema, buried inside a vast depot on a space station. The cinema was the first built for leisure, but the station has grown so tall and dense that almost no one knows it remains. Above them, pallet carriers rumble. Getting here has been hard; the belts are made for ore, not people. Stan remembers a different time, when the mag-line carried everyone—fast, smooth, and quiet—sliding past views of Falon, the planet below: clouds by day and spark-lit cities at night. As a boy he rode for hours, dodging guards, learning each curve and station. It connected everything and everyone.

Now the corporations own sectors and set boundaries for tariffs. The old places decay. Justice HQ sits on the bones of a First-Wave all-comers church, its spire gone, its ceramographite walls—once engraved with figures of early landers and beasts—pressed back into service behind bland polymer cladding. The original prison was ornamented too, but most of the beauty below is hidden by panels. Stan mourns what is buried and ignored. Daniel has heard it all. He has a job lined up on the far side, where green spaces and new builds promise comfort. The coming project is huge: an eighteen-kilometre solar funnel to draw power for the station and beam most of it to Falon. It will bring work for generations. It will also steal the view from Sectors 9 to 12 and tear down Sector 13 for anchor cables as wide as a ten-lane highway.

Stan unfolds a hand-drawn station map. He had made copies after sensing Daniel's distance, hiding them in places his son would never think to search. A blue lattice overlays the decks: the buried mag-line, still tracing old paths to pools, waste compactors, hospitals, this very cinema. The tunnels are collapsed or filled in, but the induction rails and coupling rings still run in an unbroken chain. These neglected nodes, he says, are like spots of decay in a growing tooth. Line them up and they become a fault.

The plan is simple and terrible. Send a pulse from the old power hub into those rails. The overcurrent will bake the couplings and craze the foundations of the First-Wave structures—already weakened by neglect—without toppling towers outright. Nothing dramatic will happen until the solar funnel comes online. Then, as the new cables load the frame, the station will split along the line that damage created. The separation will be slow, about a month—enough time to evacuate. It must fall, Stan says. The station has become a floating scab, a machine for profit that consumes its people and history. Breaking it is the only way to stop the injustice and force a reckoning.

Daniel won't do it. He doesn't see a revolt, only ordinary life: holidays, a middle class, decent homes on the far side. He refuses to sacrifice lives for his father's cause. And he has already chosen. A week earlier, under orders from Justice HQ, he searched Stan's home, found the original diagram, and set the trap.

Shutters high on the cinema wall grind open, flooding the dust with cold light. Justice officers rush in. Stan doesn't run. Daniel looks away. The system has served him well, and his family's status will rise because of what he has done. He will visit his father, he tells himself. He hopes they place Stan in the prison's older, lower tiers—where, in a few exposed bays, the ceramographite bricks carved long ago still show the heroes of the First Wave.
